data.title: What's the European crypto market like now after MiCA?
data.description:
Source: Coinhub; Compiled by: Jinse Finance Claw

Foreword

When we published the “European Cryptocurrency Report 2025,” there were only 12 licensed crypto asset servicers (CASPs) in the EU. Now, that number has reached 183, a 15-fold increase in 14 months. Some of our predictions have come true, some haven't; and some have been completely unexpected.

  • MiCA (Markets in Crypto-Assets, the European Crypto Asset Market Regulation Act) reduced the number of VASPs from 3,167 to 183 CASPs, but retained approximately 86% of market access through a “passporting” system.

    Introduction

    When we published the “European Cryptocurrency Report 2025,” there were only 12 licensed crypto asset servicers (CASPs) in the EU. Now, that number has reached 183, a 15-fold increase in 14 months.

    MiCA has divided the market in two: local banks with narrow business scopes, and "passport-intensive" companies covering almost the entire EU. Germany leads in the number of licenses. Cyprus and Ireland lead in passport coverage. Offshore companies have captured the biggest share of MiCA's benefits. Binance, Bybit, OKX, Crypto.com, and Revolut are at the heart of the new landscape. Bitpanda stands out as the most obvious winner among European domestic exchanges. It is the only truly EU-based cryptocurrency exchange in the global top 100. Lithuania experienced a 99% cleanup. Approximately 345 registered companies plummeted to just 4 MiCA CASP licensees. Poland is in a regulatory impasse. Over 1,400 VASPs have built around an outdated framework, with no replacement legislation forthcoming. The European regulated exchange market is extremely small. Of the 183 CASPs, only 14 are qualified to operate centralized exchanges. USDT has been squeezed out of regulated EU trading venues, but Tether continues to grow. Euro stablecoins are now part of the European sovereign advancement strategy. For EMT (electronic money token) intensive businesses, MiCA typically also means compliance with PSD2 (Payment Services Directive). July 1, 2026, is the deadline for businesses still in the "grandfathering" transition period. The EU has shifted to regulation led by ESMA (European Securities and Markets Authority). The first version of the MiCA model was tightened before the end of the first cycle.

  • Deutsche Börse's investment in Kraken shows the direction of institutional momentum.

Passporting Data

The headline news of 2025 was the end of VASPs: 3,167 national registrations collapsed. While this did happen, we underestimated the regulatory role of the passporting system in data.

Before MiCA, these 3,167 registrations were single-country and isolated. Under MiCA, 183 licensed CASPs each had access to 30 EU/EEA (European Economic Area) markets.

When calculating actual "country-access pairs," there were 2,726 pairs under MiCA, compared to 3,167 pairs previously. This means that 86% market coverage was provided through a very small number of entities, representing the pre-MiCA period. The market contracted significantly in the number of entities, but remained almost flat in actual access.

Two Markets

MiCA has created two completely different groups:

  1. 78 CASPs (42%) cover only 1 to 5 countries—almost all of them are German banks that have added crypto services to their existing compliance infrastructure. MiCA is just a marginal cost for them.

    1. 78 CASPs (42%) cover only 1 to 5 countries—almost all of them are German banks that have added crypto services to their existing compliance infrastructure. MiCA is just a marginal cost for them.

    2. 84 CASPs (45%) cover 26 to 30 countries—possessing full EU credentials, mostly crypto-native companies, and disproportionately comprised of offshore entities (which hire EU lawyers and register their credentials within the single market). Only 13 CASPs fall in the middle. This “hollow” phenomenon was unexpected.

    3. 84 CASPs (45%) cover 26 to 30 countries—possessing full EU credentials, mostly crypto-native companies, and disproportionately composed of offshore entities (which hire EU lawyers and register their credentials within the single market). ...p>84 CASPs (45%) cover 26 to


    The Real Winners

    Germany has 55 CASPs (30% of the total), but only 23 of them are traditional banks, with each license covering an average of only 6 countries.

    Cyprus has an average of 29.1 countries covered per CASP, and Ireland has 25.0. While these two countries have fewer entities than Germany, they are the true passport powerhouses.

    Cyprus has an average of 29.1 countries covered per CASP, and Ireland has 25.0.

    The names holding these Cypriot and Irish licenses include Binance (Cayman Islands), Bybit (UAE), OKX (Seychelles), Crypto.com (Cayman Islands), and Revolut (UK). These are not European companies. MiCA was not designed to hand over the most valuable licenses to offshore giants, but that's exactly what happened. Malta deserves special attention. In July 2025, ESMA's rapid peer review of Malta's CASP license found that the assessment process should have been more rigorous and that substantial issues remained unresolved when granting the license. This has turned vague suspicions about a "lax center" into clear regulatory issues and provided political leverage for France, Italy, and Austria to push for regulatory centralization.

    The Ghost of "Cheap Registration"

    Lithuania had 345 registered crypto companies at the end of 2024, thanks to five years of fintech-friendly policies: fast onboarding, low fees, and low substantive requirements. This model worked extremely well, which is also why it failed under MiCA. This model attracted companies that could not afford the minimum capital of €50,000-€150,000, mandatory physical substantive requirements, and 3-6 month review periods. Today, Lithuania has only 4 MiCA CASP licenses. The number of VASPs has plummeted by 99% from 345.

    Its first MiCA CASP is Robinhood Europe UAB—an American company. Poland, which once had over 1,800 VASPs, is now the only EU member state that hasn't incorporated MiCA into its national law. This has effectively created a political deadlock. Prime Minister Tusk's bill passed parliament but was vetoed by President Navrodsky; it was revised and resubmitted, only to be vetoed again in February 2026. Last week, parliament attempted to overturn the veto, but lost by 20 votes. There is currently no timetable for legislation. Those 1,800+ companies are "stateless" in a regulatory sense; they are too small to qualify for MiCA and too old for the old system. These companies are worth close to zero euros. Furthermore, Zonda, Poland's largest exchange, has been lobbying against the bill, while Tusk accuses it of links to Russian criminal networks and involvement in a controversial $330 million wallet linked to the former CEO who disappeared in 2022. Estonia has practically invented EU crypto regulation. It once held 37 licenses and was the first country to implement MiCA in July 2024, but currently has no CASPs on the ESMA's public register. However, this is a process: existing FIU licenses are valid until July 1, 2026, and the FSA's approval queue is moving. But this country, which built the regulatory prototype, currently has fewer publicly disclosed MiCA authorizations than even Luxembourg. Romania, which lacked crypto infrastructure before MiCA, achieved the opposite result. It progressed rapidly in transitional recognition. Crucially, Romanian companies receiving "grandfather clause" recognition do not have passport rights—ESMA has explicitly stated that transitional entities cannot cross borders. Romania gains registrations and tax revenue, while other countries bear the regulatory burden.

    Rarely Mentioned Numbers

    The ESMA register lists 183 CASPs. But only 14 are authorized to operate centralized exchanges (trading platforms with actual order books and matching engines).

    Of the 183 licensed entities, only 7% can operate what the public calls "crypto exchanges."

    The same concentration is also seen in the stablecoin space, where MiCA has reshaped market access at the token level.

    USDT "Dead" in Europe? Tether Doesn't Care

    Coinbase Europe delisted USDT in December 2024. Binance followed suit on March 31, 2025. Kraken, OKX, and Revolut followed suit. The world's largest stablecoin disappeared from all regulated EU exchanges.

    Globally, this had almost no impact. Tether's $138 billion market capitalization remained stable, with Asian markets absorbing the trading volume. In fact, daily trading volume increased after the European restrictions took effect.

    Globally, this had almost no impact.

    Tether's $138 billion market capitalization remained stable, with Asian markets absorbing the trading volume.

    ... The EU has expressed its position, but primarily directed liquidity towards Singapore and Hong Kong. Within the EU, USDC has become the preferred stablecoin for the US dollar, with EURC surging in April 2025—partly due to MiCA compliance and partly due to US tariffs reducing the attractiveness of dollar assets. However, despite the existing euro stablecoin ecosystem, its size remains a fraction of USDT's global standing. The political winds are shifting towards "sovereign" territories. The French finance minister has called for an increase in euro-pegged stablecoins and welcomed the launch of a joint euro stablecoin by ING, UniCredit, and BNP Paribas in the second half of 2026. MiCA has yet to answer the question: Can Europe create a euro alternative that people are willing to use sustainably? Who truly benefits from MiCA? Coincub's 2025 Crypto Asset Risk Report ranks all EU countries 22nd in regulatory rankings. MiCA has created a compliance bottleneck. The compliance cost floor (legal and setup fees of €50,000-€100,000, plus capital requirements, compliance personnel, and banking relationships) is viable for a Cayman entity backed by $500 million in venture capital, but absolutely unfeasible for pre-seed startups in Europe. Of the top 100 exchanges by global traffic, only eight are EU-native brands. Bitpanda is the only EU-native exchange in the upper half of the board. The compliance jobs created by banks like DekaBank, DZ Bank, and Commerzbank are real, but they are not innovative. Startups that should have hired engineers have either moved to Dubai, gone offshore and relied on reverse solicitation, or gone out of business. Europe is losing blockchain graduates. MiCA: More Than One License For EMT-intensive businesses, one license is never enough. The European Banking Authority (EBA) intervened twice on the PSD2 overlap issue (June 2025 and February 2026) because CASPs trading electronic currency tokens could also fall into the payment services sector. Prior to the second opinion, more than 100 CASPs had contacted national authorities. These companies' story was: "Get MiCA first, then deal with PSD2." The same names have consistently won because they have group structures capable of absorbing the costs of dual compliance, which eliminates smaller players. The impending breaking point: July 1st. The "grandfather clause" transition period will end on July 1, 2026. At that time, all entities not authorized by MiCA must cease operations. Poland has no way to proceed; approvals in Estonia are still pending; and the regulatory designation in Romania remains "pending publication." The peak of 45 licenses issued in December 2025 demonstrates the backlog clearing driven by deadlines. This pattern is about to repeat itself, and this time without extensions. From preparation to submission takes 9-18 months, and for those still in the transition phase and haven't submitted their applications, the window is effectively closed. ESMA Takes Over Everything The European Commission's Market Integration Plan, released in December 2025, proposes transferring all direct oversight of CASPs from national authorities to the ESMA in Paris, meaning unified authorization, monitoring, and enforcement. The European Central Bank (ECB) formally endorsed this move on April 9, 2026, spearheaded by France, Italy, and Austria. Countries like Malta are strongly resisting, as they have built competitive advantages under the "centralized model," which are now being told these advantages will be eliminated. The "license leasing" and "white label" models built on a loose, centralized foundation are directly threatened. While the ECB's opinion is not binding and negotiations will continue for months, the decentralized regulatory model that launched MiCA is already crumbling before the first version is even finalized. Institutional Signals Last week, Deutsche Bank acquired a 1.5% stake in Kraken for $200 million, valuing the company at $13.3 billion. Kraken confirmed on the same day that it had filed a confidential listing application with the SEC. The partnership covers trading, custody, settlement, and tokenized assets. The "accelerated rather than stagnant" institutional adoption story we described in our 2025 forecast review found its clearest expression in Frankfurt. The convergence of traditional finance and cryptocurrency is underway, but a European-based exchange has yet to capture this wave. Digital Euro Our 2025 report mentioned a launch of the digital euro in October 2025. This is inaccurate, as there was never a mandatory launch target in 2025. In October 2025, the ECB ended the preparatory phase and moved to the next phase. If legislation is passed in 2026, pilot programs could begin in mid-2027. The realistic launch date is 2029. The project is still progressing, slower than the ECB anticipated, and is increasingly framed as a “sovereign response” to US card organizations and Chinese payment platforms. What MiCA is and is not MiCA has fulfilled its mission, and many critics owe ESMA an apology. MiCA provided legal certainty, consumer protection standards, a public accountability registry, no expiration dates, a pass structure, and service code segmentation—truly excellent regulatory design. However, the unintended consequence is a single crypto market increasingly served by US companies, Asian entities with Cayman Islands email addresses, and German retail banks that treat crypto as a compliance checkbox, rather than by the European startups nominally intended to be nurtured by the Digital Finance Strategy. Therefore, from one perspective, MiCA is a victory for the regulatory architecture; but whether it represents a success for economic policy may largely depend on how things unfold in the coming months. In conclusion, the story of European crypto regulation is entering its most impactful chapter.

data.title: Yang Haipo: A Brief Discussion on the Base Interest Rate in Perpetual Contract Funding Rates
data.description:
Author: Yang Haipo, Founder and CEO of ViaBTC & CoinEx; Source: X, @yhaiyang

The funding rate for perpetual contracts on cryptocurrency exchanges includes a base interest rate of approximately 0.01%. This rate is charged three times daily, equivalent to an annualized rate of approximately 10%. This means that even if there is no basis between the contract price and the spot price, the market will still charge a positive funding rate of 10% annualized for long positions.

This rate has two effects. On the one hand, it provides a base return of 5% to 10% annualized for funding rate arbitrageurs in the market. The reason it doesn't reach the full 10% is because arbitrage inherently involves leverage—you need to buy the spot market while simultaneously shorting the contract, resulting in a capital utilization rate that cannot reach 100%. Depending on the trader's leverage ratio and risk management preferences, the final annualized return typically falls between 5% and 10%. On the other hand, this fee is essentially a continuous "tax" for long positions. While the percentage charged per transaction may seem low, for frequent traders, the annual interest cost after adding leverage is actually very high, sometimes exceeding the principal itself. So, where does this figure come from? In reality, many people have never delved into the underlying reasons, and even the designers of most exchanges today, such as Binance and OKX, may not understand the original intention behind setting this fee rate. This is because, essentially, most products in the industry are copied from each other, and product managers don't understand why such a mechanism exists. Smaller exchanges, lacking autonomy, can only passively align with larger exchanges; and large exchanges initially imitated BitMEX. As for why BitMEX set this figure, we need to go back to the market structure at the time. Today, USDT is widely used as margin, while BitMEX used BTC as margin in 2017 and 2018. Under that mechanism, going long implied a need to borrow USD, and going short implied a need to borrow BTC. At that time, BitMEX mainly referenced Bitfinex's interest rate market. On Bitfinex, the annualized interest rate for borrowing USD was around 10%, while the interest rate for borrowing BTC was close to zero, because the market was dominated by long positions and short positions were relatively few. Therefore, BitMEX introduced this approximately 10% annualized base interest rate and dynamically adjusted it according to market conditions. Although the market structure and interest rate environment have changed dramatically since then, this basic funding rate has been copied and retained. To put it bluntly, everyone has long forgotten the original logic in the process of copying each other, and no one dares to change this core value. More importantly, under the current USDT standard, following the same logic, the "correct" base interest rate should equal the USDT lending rate minus the lending rate of the underlying asset. USDT's interest rate in mainstream lending markets is only three or four basis points, while assets like BTC and ETH have staking and on-chain native rewards, meaning their lending rates are not zero. Subtracting the two, the theoretical base interest rate should be far below 10%, and for some assets, it might even be negative. The problem isn't just the source of the value; under the new margin system, even the direction is wrong. Moreover, this erroneous parameter is itself locked in by arbitrage funds. Funding rates should be a dynamic, market-converged mechanism. An artificially preset non-zero base interest rate is equivalent to adding a fixed-direction bias—this is no longer a convergence mechanism, but a transfer payment disguised as an interest rate. The recipients are delta-neutral arbitrage funds: long spot + short futures, profiting from the difference between the base interest rate and the lending cost. This portion of funds accumulates and is layered on exchange rates, staking rewards, and USDe (a synthetic US dollar), making the base interest rate no longer a parameter but a constant priced into countless balance sheets. Ethena's sUSDE, various USD-based "financial management" products, and market makers' carry strategies all implicitly assume this 10% will continue to exist. Whoever changes it first is the disruptor: lowering it leads to arbitrage funds withdrawing, contract depth collapsing, market maker spreads widening, and users migrating directly to counterparties that don't change it. Therefore, the rational strategy is for no one to change it. However, objectively speaking, this figure does indeed lay the foundation for a basic annualized rate of return for the entire crypto market. Around this funding rate, not only has a huge amount of arbitrage funds and related asset management projects and businesses emerged, but token projects such as USDe have also been developed. These derivative innovations are essentially financial management methods built on this base interest rate. It is no longer just an exchange parameter, but the default "crypto dollar risk-free rate"—an artificial anchor solidified by plagiarism, fueled by arbitrage funds, and maintained through collusion among all participants.

data.title: Bitcoin is within reach of $80,000. Where will this rebound lead?
data.description:
Shaw, Jinse Finance

Cryptocurrencies saw another significant rebound this morning. Bitcoin briefly touched $79,488, a more than 2% increase in 24 hours, just shy of the $80,000 mark; Ethereum briefly broke through $2,400, reaching $2,404.42, a more than 3.7% increase in 24 hours. Other major cryptocurrencies also saw slight rebounds.

Coinglass data shows that in the past 24 hours, a total of $217 million in positions were liquidated across the network, with $35.6984 million in long positions and $181 million in short positions, primarily short positions. Bitcoin short positions liquidated $70.5778 million, and Ethereum short positions liquidated $80.3837 million. In the past 24 hours, more than 74,800 people across the network have been liquidated.

Iran has conveyed a three-phase negotiation plan to the US through intermediaries. Iranian Foreign Minister Araqchi has handed over Iran's conditions for ending the war to Pakistan. Senator Thom Tillis, who previously stated he would block the confirmation of Walsh's nomination for Federal Reserve Chair, is now preparing to proceed with the Senate confirmation process. These constitute macro-positive factors boosting the market. Betting on Bitcoin's closing price in April is being conducted on the prediction market Polymarket. Traders predict an 8% probability of Bitcoin reaching $85,000 by the end of April, a 29% probability of reaching $82,500, and a 74% probability of reaching $80,000.

The market is currently focused on the progress of the US-Iran negotiations. In addition, the global financial market will usher in an extremely rare "super central bank week" this week.What will be the future trend of major risk assets, and how far can this round of rebound in the crypto market go?

I. Crypto Market Rebounds Again, Bitcoin Nears $80,000

The crypto market remained volatile for most of the weekend, but starting Sunday evening, cryptocurrencies saw a significant rebound.

Bitcoin surged this morning, briefly breaking through $79,000 and reaching $79,488, a 24-hour increase of over 2%, just shy of the $80,000 mark. Ethereum also rose, briefly breaking through $2,400 and reaching $2,404.42, a 24-hour increase of over 3.7%. Other major cryptocurrencies also saw slight rebounds. According to Coinglass data, in the past 24 hours, a total of $217 million in positions were liquidated across the entire network, with $35.6984 million in long positions and $181 million in short positions, primarily short positions. Specifically, $70.5778 million in Bitcoin short positions were liquidated, and $80.3837 million in Ethereum short positions were liquidated. Furthermore, in the past 24 hours, over 74,800 people across the network have been liquidated, with the largest single liquidation order occurring on Binance - ETHUSDT, valued at $11.9852 million. On the news front, Iran stated that it had conveyed a three-phase negotiation plan to the US through intermediaries. Iranian Foreign Minister Araqchi handed over Iran's conditions for ending the war to Pakistan. Trump stated that the war with Iran would end soon and that Iran could call the US if it wanted to negotiate. Additionally, Senator Thom Tillis, who had previously explicitly stated he would block the confirmation of Walsh's nomination for Federal Reserve Chair, has made concessions and is prepared to proceed with the Senate confirmation process. The Federal Reserve will release its final interest rate decision as Chair of the Fed this Thursday. The renewed breakthrough in US-Iran negotiations and new developments in the Fed Chair nomination constitute macro-positive factors for the market. Coupled with continued structural improvements in the market, such as ETF flows, this has led to a significant rebound in the cryptocurrency market. II. New Progress in US-Iran Talks Boosts Market Sentiment According to Iran's Tasnim News Agency on the 26th, Iranian Foreign Minister Araqchi traveled to Pakistan that day for consultations. In addition to bilateral talks, Araqchi conveyed Iran's conditions for ending the war to Pakistan, including the implementation of a new legal regime in the Strait of Hormuz, compensation, assurances against future military aggression, and the lifting of the maritime blockade. These conditions are unrelated to the nuclear issue. According to CCTV News, Iranian sources stated that Iran has conveyed a three-stage negotiation plan to the US through intermediaries. The first stage focuses on a complete end to the war and guarantees against renewed war against Iran and Lebanon. If an agreement is reached in the first stage, the parties will proceed to the second stage, which will specifically address the management of the Strait of Hormuz. The third stage relates to discussions on the nuclear issue, but Iran will not participate in nuclear negotiations until an agreement is reached in the first two stages. Reuters, citing two Pakistani government sources, reported that the Iranian delegation departed Pakistan on Saturday. US President Trump stated on Saturday that he had canceled plans to send US envoys Vitkov and Kushner to Pakistan after the Iranian chief negotiator left Islamabad after only meeting with Pakistani officials. In an interview with Fox News, Trump said he believed the war with Iran would end quickly and that the US would win. He added that if Iran wanted to negotiate, it could call the US, and expressed hope that Iran would "be smart." However, an Iranian diplomatic official indicated that a second round of talks between the Iranian and US delegations might be held in the coming days. It is understood that Iran has conveyed a message to the US, urging President Trump to reduce threatening rhetoric and stating that if the US position softens, hardliners in Iran are more likely to support participation in the negotiations. Furthermore, according to AXIOS, citing a US official and two sources familiar with the matter, Iran has made a new proposal to the US, suggesting prioritizing an agreement on reopening the Strait of Hormuz and lifting the maritime blockade, while postponing nuclear negotiations to a later stage. This new proposal aims to break the current deadlock in negotiations and bypass disagreements within the Iranian leadership regarding the scope of nuclear concessions they are willing to make to reach an agreement with the Trump administration. According to three US officials, President Trump is expected to hold a situation room meeting on Iran with his senior national security and foreign policy team on Monday. One official stated that the meeting is expected to discuss the current stalemate in negotiations with Iran and possible next steps in the war. The constant reversals in US-Iran negotiations have become the norm, but overall, progress is still ongoing. Any positive developments can boost market sentiment and risk appetite. III. The Fed's Latest Decision is Coming, Warsh's Probability of Successful Nomination Greatly Increased. This Thursday, the Federal Reserve's FOMC will conclude its two-day meeting and announce its interest rate decision. The market has already fully priced in maintaining interest rates in the 3.50% to 3.75% range. After a rate cut in the second half of 2025, the Fed will pause, and rising oil prices have further complicated the inflation outlook, pushing back the window for further rate cuts. This will also be Powell's last press conference as Fed Chairman. His term as Chairman will expire on May 15th, and Trump's nominee, Warsh, is expected to be confirmed by the Senate before the Fed's next meeting in mid-June. The biggest question now is whether Powell will announce that he will remain a member of the Fed's Board of Governors after stepping down as Chairman. Furthermore, Senator Thom Tillis, who previously stated he would block Warsh's confirmation as Fed Chairman, has made concessions and is prepared to proceed with the Senate confirmation process. Tillis had previously stated firmly that he would not approve Warsh's nomination as long as the Justice Department did not cease its investigation into Powell. However, last Friday, U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia, Piro, announced the cessation of the investigation into Federal Reserve Chairman Powell's construction costs. Thom Tillis's concession paved the way for the confirmation of Kevin Warsh's nomination as Federal Reserve Chairman. The probability of Warsh being ultimately confirmed as Federal Reserve Chairman has risen on the prediction market Polymarket, reaching 93% by May 15th. Powell's term is nearing its end. With Warsh's increased probability of being nominated, his inclinations regarding the Fed's future direction and monetary policy will have a significant impact on the market. Warsh's relatively "friendly" attitude towards cryptocurrencies and blockchain technology will also have a positive impact on the crypto market. Fourth, the US military's operation of a Bitcoin node is of great significance to cryptography. General Samuel Paparo, commander of the US Indo-Pacific Command, admitted at a hearing of the US House Armed Services Committee that the US military is running a Bitcoin node, but not for mining. Instead, it's used to monitor the network and conduct a series of operational tests to ensure the security of networks using the Bitcoin protocol. Paparo stated that the US military is using Bitcoin nodes to protect network security, and its interest in Bitcoin lies in its potential as a cryptographic tool, blockchain, and reusable proof-of-work mechanism, serving as an auxiliary means of ensuring network security and projecting power. He also stated that Bitcoin has a direct impact on national security and supports any measures that help maintain the dollar's global dominance. Galaxy commented that while the US military's operation of a Bitcoin node has no impact on the network itself, its symbolic significance is immense. Seeing such a high-ranking officer make such positive remarks should be considered a pivotal moment in the history of Bitcoin's adoption. As Galaxy stated, the remarks made by senior US military leaders regarding Bitcoin are themselves an affirmation of cryptocurrency and blockchain technology, which has profound implications for the global popularization of cryptocurrency and will stimulate a rebound in the crypto market in the short term. V. Market Structure Continues to Improve, Including ETF Flows Dune data shows that the total on-chain holdings of US spot Bitcoin ETFs have exceeded 1.66 million BTC, currently reaching approximately 1.664 million BTC, accounting for 8.31% of the current BTC supply, with an on-chain value of approximately $169 billion. SoSoValue data shows that spot Bitcoin ETFs continue to record inflows, with a net inflow of $823.7 million last week, marking the fourth consecutive week of positive inflows. Last week, the US spot Ethereum ETF saw a net inflow of $155 million. Furthermore, according to Arkham monitoring data, the on-chain wallet address of Morgan Stanley Bitcoin Trust (MSBT), a spot Bitcoin exchange-traded fund managed by Morgan Stanley, increased its holdings by 120.413 BTC last Saturday, worth $9.35 million. Currently, MSBT's total Bitcoin holdings have reached 1834 BTC, worth approximately $142 million. Bitwise data shows that in the past five trading days, US spot Bitcoin ETFs have absorbed a total of 18,991 Bitcoins, nine times the new supply from mining during the same period (2109 BTC). The continued net inflow of ETF funds, along with the continued absorption of market supply by institutional investors such as Morgan Stanley, constitutes the main structural support and positive factor for the recent crypto market, contributing to the market rebound to some extent.

VI. Market Analysis and Interpretation

Given the continuously improving macroeconomic factors and market structure, what will be the future trend of cryptocurrencies and other risky assets? Let's take a look at the main market analyses and interpretations.

1. Dominick John, analyst at Zeus Research, stated: "The rise in the crypto market was driven by stable ETF inflows and a breakout of key technical levels, which reinforced the strong buying pressure in the market. At the same time, the easing of geopolitical tensions reduced risk aversion, and the Fear & Greed Index also rose from 'Fear' to neutral. This indicates that the market is gradually returning to a more rational risk appetite."

2. Nick Ruck, Research Director at LVRG Research, believes that this rise stemmed from a recovery in risk appetite, a technical breakout, and position adjustments after re-establishing a foothold at a key resistance level.

2. Nick Ruck, Research Director at LVRG Research, believes that this rise originated from a recovery in risk appetite, a technical breakout, and position adjustments after re-establishing a foothold at a key resistance level.

3. VanEck analysts Matthew Sigel and Patrick Bush wrote that Bitcoin has shown two historically bullish signals: negative funding rates and a concentrated decline in hash rate, while volatility has cooled. With the easing of US-Iran tensions, Bitcoin's realized volatility fell from 56% to 41%, while the 7-day average funding rate turned negative to -1.8%, the lowest level since 2023. Historically, negative funding rates have typically foreshadowed strong future returns: since 2020, the 30-day average Bitcoin return during periods of negative funding rates has been +11.5%, while the overall return has been +4.5%, a hit rate of 77%. Funding rates below -5% delivered a +19.4% return over a 30-day period.

data.title: Sui-Based Scallop Protocol Hit by Flash Loan Exploit as Attacker Drains $142K From Old Rewards Contract Using Hidden Bug
data.description:
Flash Loan Attack Hits Scallop But Leaves Core Protocol Untouched

A precise exploit on the Sui-based lending platform Scallop has exposed a hidden weakness in its older code, draining around 150,000 SUI (roughly $142,000) from a deprecated rewards contract while leaving the main protocol intact.

The attacker avoided the core lending system entirely, instead targeting a sidelined contract linked to the sSUI rewards pool.

Scallop confirmed that all user deposits remain safe, with losses isolated to that single component.

🚨 Scallop hit by flash loan exploit on Sui, loses $142,000 in oracle manipulation attack

DETAILS 👇

WHAT HAPPENED?

> On April 26, 2026, the Scallop lending protocol experienced a flash loan exploit targeting a deprecated side contract related to its sSUI spool rewards pool

>… pic.twitter.com/xoZbLzGCf0

— Sophia Hodlberg (@sophiaHodlberg) April 26, 2026

How Did The Attacker Find The Weak Spot

Rather than exploiting active infrastructure, the attacker interacted with an outdated V2 contract deployed in November 2023.

Although no longer in use, the contract remained callable on-chain due to Sui’s design, where all deployed versions stay permanently accessible.

This allowed the attacker to bypass updated safeguards and operate through a neglected entry point.

Analysts believe this level of precision suggests deep familiarity with the protocol’s architecture or extensive reverse engineering.

Hidden Reward Logic Flaw Turned Small Stake Into Massive Claim

The real issue sat inside the rewards calculation system of the deprecated contract.

A key variable, `last_index`, was never properly initialised when new staking accounts were created. In normal operation, this value tracks when a user begins staking to ensure fair reward distribution over time.

Scallop drained for 150K SUI by someone who knew exactly which deprecated package to call. Not the active code. Not the SDK path. An old V2 from November 2023 that nobody's used in months. Either deep reverse engineering, or someone who knew where to look. The bug had been… pic.twitter.com/jsPE9OCsNJ

— Vadim (AI, ⋈) (@zacodil) April 26, 2026

Because it defaulted to zero, the system incorrectly treated new users as if they had been active since the pool’s inception.

With the spool index having grown to around 1.19 billion over 20 months, this misalignment allowed reward calculations to spiral far beyond intended limits.

How 136K sSUI Became A Full Pool Drain

The attacker staked roughly 136,000 sSUI and triggered the flawed logic.

The contract then credited them with around 162 trillion reward points due to the inflated historical calculation.

Since the rewards system operated on a direct 1:1 conversion model, those points translated into roughly 150,000 SUI, effectively draining the entire rewards pool.

On-chain activity shows the funds were quickly routed through a mixing service, reducing the chance of recovery.

Was This Oracle Manipulation Or A Legacy Code Failure

While some early discussion pointed toward price manipulation, the exploit appears more rooted in legacy accounting logic than direct oracle tampering.

The attacker did not need to break active price feeds or core lending mechanics.

Instead, they exploited how outdated reward data was still being processed by an accessible contract version.

This separation between active and deprecated systems is what allowed the exploit to remain contained to a single pool.

Why Deprecated Contracts Remain A Persistent Risk

On Sui, deployed contracts are immutable and remain on-chain unless explicitly restricted.

That design means older versions can still be interacted with even after newer systems replace them.

In Scallop’s case, the V2 rewards module was effectively forgotten in production terms but still technically live, creating an exposed edge in the system.

The "side contract" framing undersells it. The bug was in a deprecated package nobody's used in months - left immutable on Sui after upgrades. Either deep reverse engineering of every Scallop package ever shipped, or insider knowledge of exactly where the bug was. Full on-chain…

— Vadim (AI, ⋈) (@zacodil) April 26, 2026

This type of “stale package” risk is now being flagged more frequently across DeFi, where legacy components are left callable without ongoing monitoring.

Rapid Containment And Full Recovery Promise

Scallop responded by freezing the affected contract within minutes of detection and temporarily pausing operations tied to the rewards pool.

Core lending and borrowing markets were not impacted, and user funds remained untouched throughout.

Operations resumed within roughly two hours after the incident, with deposits and withdrawals restored to normal.

The team also confirmed it will fully cover the loss from its treasury, preventing any impact on user balances or yields.

✅ INCIDENT UPDATE

We have unfreezed the core contracts and all operations have resumed. The issue was not related to the core protocol and was isolated to a deprecated rewards contract.

User deposits were not impacted and all funds remain safe. Withdrawals and deposits are now…

— Scallop (@Scallop_io) April 26, 2026

Audit Questions Resurface After Exploit

The affected contract had previously been reviewed in audits from firms including OtterSec and MoveBit, raising questions over how the vulnerability persisted.

Scallop has indicated a full post-mortem will examine why the flaw was not detected earlier and how deprecated modules are handled in future reviews.

Analysts have pointed out that audits often focus on active systems, while legacy contracts may receive less continuous scrutiny despite remaining live on-chain.

Growing Pattern Of Peripheral Exploits Across Sui

The incident adds to a recent string of attacks on Sui-based protocols where attackers have increasingly targeted side contracts rather than core systems.

Similar patterns were seen in other DeFi incidents this year, where unused modules, adapters, or legacy infrastructure were exploited instead of primary lending logic.

another defi protocol hacked 😱

this time it's @scallop_io - one of sui's largest defi protocols

this is the 4th hack of any @SuiNetwork ecosystem dapp in last one year & 2nd this week.

1. cetus hack (may 22, 2025) - $223m
2. nemo (sep 7, 2025) - ~$2.4m
3. volo (apr 21, 2026)… https://t.co/YUqcgX6dmTpic.twitter.com/1YgVtgJbpV

— Observe (@obsrvgmi) April 26, 2026

This shift suggests attackers are focusing on overlooked edges of ecosystems rather than heavily audited core contracts.

Market Reaction Stays Calm Despite Exploit

Despite the breach, SUI price remained stable and even rose slightly by nearly 2% in the following 24 hours, trading around $0.94.

Trading activity also held steady at approximately $187 million in daily volume, suggesting limited market concern over systemic risk.

Investigation Now Focuses on Legacy Contract Management

Attention has now shifted to how deprecated contracts are managed across DeFi protocols.

With immutable deployments leaving old code permanently accessible, the key question is how teams can prevent unused systems from becoming hidden entry points.

Scallop is expected to release a full technical breakdown of the exploit, including how the reward logic flaw went unnoticed for more than a year and how similar risks can be eliminated going forward.

data.title: Eliminating Float: Programmable Money and New Financial Guardrails
data.description:
Author: Jordi Visser, Senior Analyst at Wall Street; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

We are witnessing firsthand the subtle shifts in the safeguards of the global financial system, though these shifts are so gradual that they appear to be merely a series of isolated decisions. This is the pattern of systemic evolution: not a single, complete break, but rather the convergence of multiple trends. Over the past 18 months, four distinct companies—Stripe, Uber, DoorDash, and Morgan Stanley—have each, in different ways, laid the groundwork for the same future. Infrastructure providers, operators, platform giants, and traditional banks are reaching a consensus around a core concept: the underlying rules of money flow are being rewritten, ushering in a new era of globalized currency, near-instantaneous clearing, and programmability.

This is not simply a payment upgrade, but a fundamental transformation of the logic of currency operation. Stripe had already recognized in its past practices that a path that wouldn't work: using highly volatile cryptocurrencies as a means of everyday payment for the general public. The acquisition of Bridge in October 2024 clearly indicated its chosen direction: using stablecoins as a programmable, global underlying settlement infrastructure. At the time, most people had a narrow perspective, interpreting this move merely as targeting the cross-border remittance and cross-border payment market. But this understanding overlooked the core essence: this is not about optimizing a particular type of payment scenario, but about reconstructing the underlying transmission network upon which all payment activities rely. Bridge's core goal is to solve the fragmented pain points of global capital flows, shielding against a series of obstacles such as foreign exchange, multi-layered bank intermediaries, and settlement delays. Stripe is not chasing industry hot topics, but directly addressing long-standing structural inefficiencies: information now flows freely without borders, but capital flows are still firmly bound by geographical boundaries. At the Bloomberg Technology Conference in June 2025, this core insight was further clarified. Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi publicly defined stablecoins as a practical tool for multinational corporations. His statement is intriguing, not because of what he mentioned, but because of what he deliberately avoided: he didn't discuss speculation, decentralized concepts, or ideological controversies, focusing solely on pure business value—stablecoins can significantly reduce the cost and friction barriers of global capital flows. This sends a crucial signal: the widespread adoption of stablecoins will not be driven by ideology or belief, but by efficiency advantages. Recently, the industry saw a landmark development: DoorDash officially launched its stablecoin payroll settlement solution in over 40 countries worldwide. At first glance, this seems like a natural continuation of industry trends, another company attempting to improve payment efficiency. However, this superficial interpretation obscures the underlying transformation that is taking place. DoorDash does more than just optimize fund transfers; it reshapes the mechanism by which money operates. Essentially, DoorDash is a typical multilateral trading platform: after a consumer pays for a service, the funds need to be split and settled among the platform, the delivery person, and the participating merchants. In the traditional financial system, this process is cumbersome and slow: funds are centrally collected, circulated through multiple layers of financial intermediaries, and then distributed later by a back-end reconciliation system. The entire process involves time, intermediaries, and manual reconciliation. These shortcomings are not accidental defects, but rather deeply ingrained structural characteristics of the traditional financial system. Stablecoins completely overturn this old model, replacing it with a completely new underlying logic. Funds can be automatically split and distributed the moment they are credited to the account. In a programmable financial system, transaction rules and revenue-sharing logic are directly embedded in the payment process. The platform automatically deducts its share the moment a consumer places an order, and the corresponding income for delivery personnel and merchants is credited in real time. There is no need for centralized batch processing, no settlement delays, and no post-transaction reconciliation. Value distribution is no longer a separate, post-transaction step, but rather an integral part of the transaction itself. This is the most crucial paradigm shift we are witnessing today. In the traditional system, which relies on intermediaries and is fraught with friction, funds are first collected and then distributed later; however, in a programmable distributed financial system, funds are distributed the instant they arrive. This difference directly eliminates an entire set of redundant financial infrastructure. Traditional settlement cycles are no longer necessary, and reliance on intermediaries is significantly reduced. At the same time, it also heralds the end of the era of "floating capital"—for a long time, the inefficient window created by massive amounts of capital being tied up in transit has been a core element for banks to earn profits. Complex back-office financial systems are no longer a necessity for business operations. Most importantly, the core constraint of time in the financial system has been undermined: time is no longer a necessary condition for multi-party collaborative settlement, but has become a freely optional add-on. This is precisely the key reason why DoorDash's implementation is far more significant than previous industry moves. Stripe identifies structural inefficiencies; Uber validates commercialization scenarios; DoorDash achieves large-scale implementation of its new model. The core of this new model is never just faster payment speed, but rather **programmable money**. Its impact extends far beyond the delivery platform itself. All industries requiring fund aggregation and delayed revenue sharing—ride-hailing, e-commerce, global payroll processing, and supply chain settlement—can restructure their operating models based on this new logic. Historical pain points such as delayed settlements, internal reconciliation, and fragmented revenue sharing systems will gradually disappear, replaced by the continuous flow of value, with transactions and fund allocation completed simultaneously. The transformation of financial risk control rules is thus beginning. For decades, traditional finance has relied on procedural friction to maintain order: settlement delays allow time for reconciliation and auditing, intermediaries assume regulatory and risk control functions, and geographical boundaries strictly limit the scope of fund flows. These constraints are the underlying foundation for the stability of traditional finance. Programmable money, however, replaces all human and institutional constraints with code rules. It replaces time-difference control with instant execution rules via smart contracts; it replaces human intermediary review with embedded automated logic in transactions; and it breaks down geographical barriers to capital flows through a globally unified network. Financial risk control hasn't disappeared; it has simply migrated from institutional control to the software and code level. This rule migration has already begun in earnest. Formal endorsements from traditional financial institutions are following suit. Morgan Stanley recently launched a stablecoin reserve asset portfolio, specifically allocating underlying reserve assets for stablecoins with a compliant and highly liquid architecture; it also advised clients to allocate a small percentage of their assets to crypto assets and launched a Bitcoin spot ETF. The signal is clear: traditional finance is not standing aside from the digital asset revolution, but actively adapting and deeply integrating. The next wave of revolution is already emerging. When currency becomes programmable and flows in real time, funds will not stagnate after the initial allocation, but will enter a state of continuous dynamic management. Artificial intelligence agents can automatically handle fund flows, completing fund splitting, savings, and investment according to preset rules the moment funds arrive. After delivery personnel's income arrives in real time, a portion of the funds can be automatically transferred to investment accounts, short-term government bond funds, or other financial products. Under this system, currency not only flows faster, but also achieves intelligent and proactive management from the moment it is generated, completely blurring the boundaries between payment and asset management. For a long time, time constraints have been deeply tied to the financial system; settlement cycles, batch processing, and post-event reconciliation all rely on time buffers. In this new paradigm, however, time is no longer a rigid limitation, but a flexibly manageable option. Funds no longer need to wait for approval, have their allocation delayed, or their investment postponed; circulation, allocation, and compound interest growth are all completed instantly. The transformation that began in 2024 at the infrastructure level has now deeply penetrated the real economy and is further supported by traditional institutions. The entire financial system is not being overthrown by external forces, but rather undergoing internal restructuring and iteration. This is the true face of structural change. It won't originate from a single sudden event, but rather from a series of gradual, progressive actions: October 2024: Underlying infrastructure completed adaptation; June 2025: Leading operating companies recognize its practical value; 2026: New business models scaled up; Currently: Traditional financial institutions are fully aligning with the trend. Every decision is rational, pragmatic, and gradual, yet they all point to the same irreversible direction: Currency is becoming globalized; transactions are becoming real-time and instantaneous; and value transfer is becoming programmable. DoorDash is not merely introducing a new payment method; it is implementing a completely new financial paradigm: funds are allocated automatically and instantly based on preset rules, eliminating the need for delayed distribution. This is a fundamentally different financial system. Like all profound structural reforms, it never makes its debut with a dramatic, revolutionary gesture. At first, it seemed like just a series of insignificant, minor decisions. Until quantitative changes led to qualitative changes, and the era was completely rewritten.

data.title: Self-check: Is your company agent-friendly?
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

I. Trend: In the AI ​​Era, Comprehensive Business Integration is Unstoppable

In recent years, the global technology industry has undergone a silent but profound paradigm shift. If the past decade was the golden age of "Internet+", then the present and the next decade will be the certain future of "AI+". From the wave of large-scale models sparked by ChatGPT to the rush of various industries to deploy intelligent agents, an undeniable fact is emerging: AI is no longer a tool for adding icing on the cake, but is becoming the infrastructure for the survival and development of enterprises.

Responsibility Boundaries and Emergency Mechanisms: When the behavior of an AI agent causes losses, how should responsibility be divided? In a company's governance structure, the behavioral boundaries of the AI ​​agent, the timing of human intervention, and the post-incident handling procedures must be clearly defined.

III. Current Situation: Most Companies' Businesses Are Not Yet Ready to Connect with AI

If we examine most companies using the above four dimensions as a benchmark, we will find an embarrassing reality: companies that claim to "embrace AI" have business systems and management processes that are extremely hostile, even "antagonistic," to AI agents.

(I) From an Internal Management Perspective: The Data Swamp and the Process Maze The biggest obstacle to AI from internal management lies primarily in data quality. AI agents are not magicians; they rely on high-quality, structured, and semantically consistent data. However, the state of data within many enterprises can only be described as appalling: The same field (such as "customer name" or "product model") in different departments uses completely different naming rules and coding systems in their respective systems; Paper documents and electronic documents coexist, with a large amount of critical information locked in scanned copies, PDF images, and even audio recordings, requiring AI to perform complex OCR and speech recognition to "understand" it; Missing historical data, entry errors, and duplicate records are common, and data governance remains merely a slogan; The business processes themselves lack standardization; the same approval item may take completely different paths in different branches and with different handlers. When an AI agent attempts to access such an internal system, it faces not a clear and smooth information highway, but a data swamp full of potholes, dead ends, and dead ends. How can an AI agent that approves reimbursements automatically approve expenses if the classification logic for "travel expenses" and "transportation expenses" is inconsistent across different systems? (II) From the perspective of external business: Closed interface and arbitrary processes. External business systems also have many problems. Many companies' customer interfaces and transaction systems were not designed with the scenario of "AI agents acting as users" in mind from the outset. The lack or non-standardization of APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) is a major pain point. For example, an AI-powered procurement agent aiming to automatically compare product prices would be forced to use a "simulated browser" approach to crawl web pages if the target supplier's website lacked an open, standardized, and authenticated product information interface—a method that is fragile, inefficient, and often violates the website's terms of service. Many companies still view "external interfaces" as a technical issue rather than a strategic one, providing outdated API documentation, obsolete authentication methods, and opaque rate-limiting policies, making them extremely unfriendly to AI agents. The human-centric approach to process design is equally serious. The interfaces of most business systems are designed for human vision, human reaction time, and human attention patterns: complex multi-level menus, operation options that require "hovering" to appear, dynamically refreshed CAPTCHAs, and forced-read pop-up announcements… These designs may only be slightly inconvenient for human users, but they are insurmountable obstacles for AI agents. An AI agent aiming to automatically complete order queries will become a fragile behemoth riddled with conditional branches and exception handling when faced with a process that requires opening a three-level menu, searching for the "last 7 days" option in a vague date picker, and then encountering a satisfaction survey pop-up that may appear at any time. (III) From the perspective of ecosystem participation: fragmented efforts and lack of standards. While internal and business integration issues can be resolved through the efforts of individual companies, the challenges at the ecosystem level far exceed the control of any single enterprise. The primary problem is the heterogeneity of system architectures among enterprises. Company A's ERP and Company B's CRM have completely different underlying data models; the meanings of order status codes in one industry and logistics status codes in another are incomprehensible. When multiple intelligent agents from different enterprises attempt to collaborate on an end-to-end business process, their primary challenge is not complex business logic, but rather the fundamental problem of "translation"—a translation task that often requires extensive custom development, completely contradicting the original intention of automated collaboration. The lack or fragmentation of standards and protocols is a deeper issue. While some industry organizations and technology alliances are promoting B2B integration standards (such as certain versions of EDI, RosettaNet, OASIS, etc.), these standards are either outdated and fail to consider the autonomous interaction needs of intelligent agents; or they are too complex and costly to implement; or they have limited coverage, applicable only to specific industries or stages. Currently, an open and universal standard protocol covering the entire chain of "agent discovery - capability negotiation - data exchange - state synchronization - anomaly handling - settlement and clearing" is still lacking. Trust and security mechanisms are major obstacles to ecosystem collaboration. How can an intelligent agent from one enterprise verify the identity and authorization of another enterprise's intelligent agent? How can sensitive data during interactions be prevented from being leaked or eavesdropped on by third parties? When an intelligent agent behaves abnormally (e.g., is maliciously hijacked), how can its ecosystem access be quickly detected and severed? These problems can be partially solved under traditional API security frameworks (OAuth, API keys, mTLS, etc.), but when the interacting entity changes from an "enterprise system" to an "autonomous intelligent agent," and when the interaction mode changes from "request-response" to "multi-turn dialogue and autonomous decision-making," existing security models become inadequate. (IV) From a Governance and Compliance Perspective: Responsibility Vacuum and Regulatory Lag Compliance issues are equally worrying. Many companies, in their AI development, essentially adopt a "get it running first, then worry about the consequences" approach, with governance structures lagging significantly behind technology deployment. The attribution of responsibility for AI decisions is ambiguous—when a human resources AI agent mistakenly filters out a qualified candidate, is it the responsibility of the algorithm engineer? The business department? Or the AI ​​itself (if legally permissible)? Companies often lack clear and operational rules for assigning responsibility, ultimately resulting in no one being held accountable. The lack of ethical review mechanisms—what AI application scenarios require ethical review? Who should form the review committee? What are the review standards? Most companies simply haven't established relevant systems and processes. As a result, some AI applications with obvious risks of discrimination or privacy are launched without sufficient evaluation, only to be hastily remedied after complaints or public outcry. The "transfer mentality" of regulatory compliance—many companies directly "transfer" traditional compliance requirements to AI scenarios without understanding the new problems brought about by AI. For example, data protection regulations require users to have the right to "explain" the logic of automated decisions, but can the decision boundary of a deep neural network really be clearly "explained" in natural language? Companies often use superficial explanations such as "our model is based on three features X, Y, and Z" to evade the issue, without truly addressing the problem of explainability. IV. Action: The Inevitable Path of Comprehensive Attention Faced with the aforementioned complex situation, enterprises cannot simply passively wait for technology to mature or standards to be perfected; instead, they must proactively and systematically promote AI integration across four levels: strategy, technology, business, and compliance. (I) Strategic Level: From "Tool Thinking" to "Ecosystem Thinking" Top management needs to clearly recognize that AI is not just the next software project, but a strategic variable influencing business models and organizational structures. AI integration is not just a matter for the IT department, but for the entire company. Enterprises need to develop a clear AI strategic roadmap, specifying which business segments will introduce AI agents over the next three to five years, which agent capabilities will be developed in-house or outsourced, how to balance automation and human intervention, and how to respond to changes in the external ecosystem. More importantly, enterprises need to make a fundamental shift: from viewing AI as an internal tool for improving efficiency to viewing it as an identity carrier for participating in the external ecosystem. This means that a company's strategic planning should include considerations such as "how our agents will collaborate with agents in other organizations" and "what role we hope to play in the entire agent ecosystem." (II) Technical Aspect: Building a System Architecture "Friendly to Intelligent Agents" The technical team needs to re-examine the existing system architecture, shifting from a "human-centered" design philosophy to a "dual-center" design philosophy of "human and intelligent agent". Specifically, enterprises need to: All core business functions should be exposed through well-designed, fully documented, and version-controlled APIs, optimized for machine-to-machine call scenarios (e.g., providing batch operation interfaces, supporting asynchronous callbacks, and designing reasonable rate limiting and retry strategies). Data Readiness: Establish a unified data governance framework to ensure that key data entities (customers, products, orders, suppliers, etc.) have unique, clear, and machine-readable definitions across the company. Historical data cleaning and labeling should be treated as a strategic investment. Embedded Intelligence: Extract valuable business logic (such as approval rules, pricing strategies, and risk assessment standards) from hard-coded applications and encapsulate them into independent "capability units" that can be invoked by AI agents. This allows agents to combine these capabilities like building blocks, rather than being forced to repeatedly implement or bypass them.

Observable Design: Design sufficient logging, monitoring, and tracking capabilities for the behavior of AI agents. When multiple agents work collaboratively, it is possible to clearly trace which agent made which decision, at what time, and based on what data. This is the foundation for troubleshooting and accountability.

(III) Business Level: Redesigning Processes and Roles

Business departments cannot completely delegate AI integration to the technology department. On the contrary, the restructuring of business processes is the key to the implementation of AI.

Process Simplification and Standardization: Before integrating with AI, examine the rationality of existing processes. A process that is inherently chaotic and full of exceptions will only create more chaos after automation. Enterprises need to first streamline and standardize their business processes, reducing unnecessary branches and exceptions. Human-Machine Collaboration Model Design: Not all processes are suitable for full automation. Enterprises need to clearly define which processes will be completely handed over to AI, which require AI to process first and then human review, and which will always remain human. The key is to design smooth "transfer" and "upgrade" mechanisms, rather than simply replacing humans with AI.

Employee Empowerment: AI is not here to steal jobs, but it will certainly change the content of jobs. Enterprises need to systematically train employees, teaching them how to collaborate with AI agents, how to understand and intervene in the decision-making process of AI agents, and how to take over and handle anomalies when AI agents fail.

(IV) Compliance Level: Establishing Embedded Governance Mechanisms

Compliance should not be an afterthought, but should be embedded in the entire lifecycle of AI system design, development, deployment, and operation from day one.

Compliance should not be a post-hoc "patch", but should be embedded in the entire lifecycle of AI system design, development, deployment, and operation from day one.

data.title: a16z: Stablecoins are becoming more localized
data.description:
Authors: Robert Hackett, editor at a16z crypto; Jeremy Zhang, partner at a16z; Source: a16z crypto; Compiled by: Shaw, Jinse Finance

Stablecoins have been searching for their core value narrative for years.

Initially, they were simply a trading instrument used to transfer US dollar funds between various exchanges. Subsequently, stablecoins evolved into a savings vehicle, chosen to be held rather than used for consumption. Now, data points to a new direction: stablecoins are becoming core financial infrastructure.

The following nine charts will illustrate the underlying logic driving this trend.

1. Regulatory Implementation Boosts Market Growth For most of the stablecoin development process, regulatory uncertainty has limited institutional investment. Then, the **Genius Act** was enacted, clarifying the regulatory framework. While not the initial catalyst for this trend, the act significantly amplified the industry's growth momentum. In the United States, the **Genius Act** established the first federal regulatory framework for stablecoin issuance. This policy shift is clearly reflected in the data: even before the bill was passed, the adjusted trading volume of stablecoins had already been rising for several consecutive quarters, and the growth accelerated further after the bill was implemented, reaching approximately $4.5 trillion in the first quarter of 2026. The EU's stablecoin regulatory framework—the Crypto Asset Markets Act (MiCA)—presents a more complex picture. After the bill took full effect at the end of 2024, several major exchanges delisted Tether (USDT) for compliance, directly driving a surge in trading volume of non-USD stablecoins, exceeding $40 billion in a short period. Subsequently, trading volume of non-USD stablecoins declined and stabilized, with the overall base significantly higher than before the Crypto Asset Markets Act, and monthly volume stabilizing between $15 billion and $25 billion. Regulatory policies unexpectedly created a long-term market for non-USD stablecoins that was previously almost entirely untapped. 2. The scale of commercial applications of stablecoins continues to expand. From a long-term structural perspective, the most crucial shift may lie in the qualitative change in the actual uses of stablecoins by users. In terms of raw transaction volume, C2C (consumer-to-consumer) transactions far outpaced all other categories, reaching 789.5 million transactions in 2025. C2B (consumer-to-business) stablecoin transactions saw the fastest growth, with transaction volume increasing by 128% year-on-year, from 124.9 million transactions in 2024 to 284.6 million transactions in 2025, more than doubling in size. Data from stablecoin payment card infrastructure further confirms this trend. The monthly collateralized deposits of various stablecoin card projects (including Etherfi Cash, Kast, Wallbit, etc.) powered by Rain climbed from almost zero in November 2024 to an average of over $300 million per month in early 2026. Although this funding is used as collateral to support card spending, rather than direct stablecoin consumption, its growth momentum is remarkable: Stablecoin commercial payment scenarios are booming. 3. Stablecoin circulation velocity continues to accelerate. For every dollar of stablecoins in circulation, the frequency of fund turnover is constantly increasing. Since the beginning of 2024, the **velocity of stablecoin circulation** (the adjusted ratio of monthly transaction volume to circulating supply) has roughly doubled, climbing from 2.6 times to 6 times. This increased velocity of circulation means that the growth rate of stablecoin transaction demand has exceeded the growth rate of new issuance, significantly improving the utilization efficiency of existing funds. This is a typical characteristic of mature payment networks: the underlying currency is widely used in practice, rather than simply held passively. 4. The proportion of payment attributes in stablecoin transactions continues to increase. After excluding traditional mainstream scenarios such as trading speculation, fund transfers, and internal exchanges, the actual transaction volume of cross-entity payment transactions last year reached approximately $350 billion to $550 billion. Business-to-business (B2B) transactions dominate the stablecoin payment volume (this result is not surprising given the scale of the business). However, other scenarios such as peer-to-peer (C2C) direct transfers and merchant payments are also expanding rapidly. 5. Stablecoin payments are currently highly concentrated in specific regions. From a geographical perspective, stablecoin payment activities exhibit a clear regional imbalance. Nearly two-thirds of the transaction volume comes from Asia, mainly concentrated in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Japan. North America accounts for about a quarter, and Europe accounts for about 13%. Latin America and Africa combined have a very small share, with a transaction volume of less than $1 billion. 6. More Than Just Cross-Border Payments – Local Currencies, Global Settlement Networks The rise of non-dollar stablecoins is not unique to Europe; emerging markets are also showing significant momentum, with different driving factors behind each. Brazil is a prime example. **BRLA is a stablecoin backed by the Brazilian Real reserve.** Its monthly transaction volume grew from almost zero at the beginning of 2023 to approximately $400 million per month at the beginning of 2026. The stablecoin has been integrated with Brazil's instant payment system, PIX, significantly promoting its widespread adoption. While stablecoins are often seen as a cross-border tool, the proportion of cross-border transactions has actually been **continuously declining**, rather than increasing. The proportion of domestic transactions climbed from about half of the total payment volume in early 2024 to nearly three-quarters in early 2026. What does this mean? The development positioning of stablecoins is changing: they are no longer limited to remittance and foreign exchange tools, but are gradually becoming local daily payment mediums based on global underlying infrastructure. Conclusion: Based on various data, a clear industry picture has emerged, which is quite different from the common predictions of the public: in the past, most people believed that the core value of stablecoins would focus on cross-border transfers. But the reality is quite the opposite; stablecoins are accelerating their localization. Currently, the US dollar remains the pegged fiat currency for the vast majority of stablecoins, holding an absolute dominant position, but stablecoins are not simply products of US dollar exports. Stablecoins using non-US dollar fiat currencies such as the euro and the Brazilian real are rapidly gaining market share. Furthermore, while the number of peer-to-peer stablecoin transactions still far exceeds that of other payment types, their usage frequency in daily commercial consumption scenarios is steadily increasing. Quarterly data consistently confirms that stablecoins are gradually evolving into a universal payment infrastructure. While possessing global attributes in their underlying design, their actual application scenarios are becoming increasingly localized. The industry is still in its early stages of development, but the outline of this new financial system is becoming increasingly clear.

data.title: Goldman Sachs: What does DeepSeek V4 mean for AI in China?
data.description:
Author: Bao Yilong, Wall Street Insights

Goldman Sachs believes thatThe core significance of DeepSeek V4 lies in supporting the deployment of more complex intelligent agent applications at a lower cost, thereby opening up new space for the large-scale application of AI. On April 24th, Goldman Sachs' Ronald Keung team published a research report stating that the newly open-source V4 model is a continuation of DeepSeek's efficiency-first, open-source approach. Technically, V4 achieves significant cost reductions in long context windows through architectural upgrades and clearly bets on Huawei's domestically produced chips. In the market, this release accelerates the fierce competition in China's AI model market, with programming ability, task completion rate, and multimodal capabilities becoming the core watershed for pricing power. Goldman Sachs maintains its "Buy" rating on the Cloud Computing and Data Center sectors. Continued improvements in computing cost efficiency will accelerate the penetration of AI applications. The dual drivers of growth in enterprise AI agents and consumer AI assistants will support the continued improvement of cloud service pricing capabilities. DeepSeek V4 is upgraded with a more robust architecture, supporting longer contexts with less memory. It is released in Pro and Flash versions. The Pro version is flagship-level, with 1.6 trillion parameters (49 billion activated); the Flash version is relatively lightweight, with 284 billion parameters (13 billion activated). Both models support ultra-long context windows of 1 million tokens, on par with top-tier (SOTA) models in the US, but with significantly reduced memory requirements and key-value (KV) cache. According to a Goldman Sachs report, in a 1 million context scenario, V4 Pro requires only 27% of the floating-point operations (FLOPs) for token inference compared to DeepSeek V3.2, and its KV cache usage is only 10%; V4 Flash is even more aggressive, reducing FLOPs to 10% and compressing the KV cache to 7%. This efficiency leap is achieved through three key architectural innovations: Regarding the hybrid attention mechanism, V4 introduces a hybrid architecture of Compressed Sparse Attention (CSA) and Heavily Compressed Attention (HCA). CSA first compresses the sequence dimension of the KV cache before performing sparse attention computation, while HCA uses more aggressive compression but retains dense attention. Together, they significantly reduce the temporary memory required for long inputs. Regarding training stability, V4 introduces the mHC mechanism to enhance the stability of information transmission in multi-layer networks; it also adopts Muon as the main training optimizer (with some modules retaining AdamW) to adapt to more complex network architectures than V3, improving the convergence quality of the training process. Goldman Sachs points out that the above efficiency gains are more significant for long-duration task scenarios, with typical use cases being long-cycle proxy tasks that require processing a large amount of context. It is worth noting that DeepSeek is currently still focused on basic text models, while internet giants such as Alibaba, ByteDance, and MiniMax, as well as independent model makers, tend to favor multimodal/full-modal approaches, showing a clear divergence in their AGI exploration paths. Domestic chip deployment accelerates, Huawei Ascend 950 paves the way for price reductions. Another important signal from this V4 release is that DeepSeek has explicitly included the mass production of Huawei Ascend 950 supernodes in its commercial strategy. DeepSeek expects that with large-scale supply of Huawei Ascend 950 supernodes in the second half of 2026, the API pricing of the V4 Pro version will see a significant decrease. Goldman Sachs' report indicates that this statement has a dual meaning: First, DeepSeek's cost competitiveness will be further strengthened, creating conditions for wider application; Second, against the backdrop of continued tightening of chip supply, the trend of top Chinese AI models migrating to domestic computing power has received clear endorsement from leading players. From the current pricing perspective, Goldman Sachs data shows that the V4 Pro is already competitively priced on mainstream API platforms, and this advantage is expected to further expand in the second half of 2026 as domestic computing power supply expands. The open-source release of DeepSeek V4 has quickly triggered a new round of intensive follow-up from the Chinese AI model camp. According to Goldman Sachs, players recently launching new models include: Kimi K2.6, Alibaba Qwen3.6-Max, Tencent Hy3 preview version, Xiaomi V2.5, and MiniMax M3/Hailuo, which is expected to be launched in May. According to Goldman Sachs, the key differentiating factors that will determine the pricing power of various models in the future will focus on two dimensions: programming/task completion success rate, where Zhipu's GLM model ranks among the top in coding ability; and multimodal capabilities, where ByteDance, Alibaba, and MiniMax have invested the most deeply. The research report points out that the advantages and disadvantages of two types of players are clear: Independent AI players, such as MiniMax, have high organizational efficiency and short decision-making chains. Even with extremely low pricing for their basic text APIs, they can still achieve a 40% gross profit margin, according to Goldman Sachs' forecast. Internet giants, such as ByteDance, Tencent, and Alibaba, have abundant cash flow from their core businesses, making them more suitable for developing AI infrastructure and cloud computing. They need to set up independent incentive programs for their AI teams to retain talent; for example, ByteDance's Doubao team already has independent incentives. It is worth noting that, according to a Goldman Sachs report citing news reports, Tencent and Alibaba are in talks to invest in DeepSeek at a valuation of over $20 billion, while Zhipu and MiniMax have the latest market capitalizations of approximately $53 billion and $31 billion, respectively. This potential deal reflects the logic of giants vying for scarce top-tier AI capabilities.
